intro: AI model releases and infrastructure concerns dominate this week's news, with OpenAI previewing GPT-5.6 Sol, Anthropic launching Claude Science, and a growing conversation about what AI is actually costing companies — and the grid.

stories.title: OpenAI Previews GPT-5.6 Sol: Stronger Coding, Science, and Cybersecurity
stories.summary: OpenAI has previewed GPT-5.6 Sol, described as a next-generation model with meaningfully improved capabilities in coding, scientific reasoning, and cybersecurity tasks. The announcement comes alongside confirmation that GPT-5.6 Sol Ultra will be integrated into Codex, OpenAI's AI-powered coding tool, which is generating significant buzz on Hacker News. Sol appears to be a step up from the existing GPT-5.5 line, with OpenAI emphasizing that it ships with their 'most advanced safety stack' to date — a sign they're trying to get ahead of concerns about increasingly capable models operating more autonomously. The cybersecurity angle is notable: models with strong offensive security knowledge are dual-use by nature, and the fact that OpenAI is flagging this as a feature rather than a liability suggests a deliberate positioning shift. For developers using Codex, having Sol Ultra in the loop could meaningfully raise the ceiling on automated code generation and review tasks. The model preview pattern OpenAI has been using — announce early, let developers plan around it — is becoming a reliable part of their release cadence. It also keeps the competitive pressure on Anthropic and Google, who are both pushing hard in the same territory. Worth watching how benchmark comparisons shake out once independent evaluations come in, since first-party capability claims should always be taken with some skepticism.

stories.title: Claude Science Is Anthropic's Newest Flagship — Built for Research the Way Claude Code Is Built for Engineering
stories.summary: Anthropic announced Claude Science at an event for pharmaceutical executives, biotech founders, and academic researchers — positioning it as the scientific research equivalent of Claude Code. Like Claude Code, Claude Science can autonomously carry out extended, meaningful work when given high-level instructions, and it has access to domain-specific tools and data sources relevant to scientific workflows. The framing is deliberate: Anthropic wants to be the AI partner for serious science, not just a chatbot that can answer biology questions. This is a significant product bet because scientific research has very different reliability requirements than software — a wrong line of code fails loudly, but a wrong hypothesis can waste months of lab time or, worse, make it into a publication. The pharmaceutical and biotech angle makes commercial sense too, since drug discovery is one of the few domains where companies have both the budget and the incentive to pay serious money for AI that actually works. Anthropic has been positioning Claude as the 'thoughtful, careful' AI compared to OpenAI's more aggressive release pace, and Claude Science fits that brand. MIT Technology Review covered the launch in some depth, noting that the announcement was made at an industry event rather than a general press conference — a sign that Anthropic is targeting enterprise science customers rather than the general public. Whether it can actually deliver on autonomous scientific reasoning at the level needed to be genuinely useful in a wet lab or computational biology pipeline remains to be seen.

stories.title: Google DeepMind Launches Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash for Developers
stories.summary: Google DeepMind pushed out two new model releases aimed squarely at developers: Nano Banana 2 Lite and Gemini Omni Flash. The naming convention alone tells you something — 'Lite' and 'Flash' are firmly in the efficiency tier, not the frontier tier. These are models optimized for speed and cost, designed for applications where you're making a lot of calls and can't afford the latency or expense of a full-scale model. Gemini Omni Flash extends the 'Omni' multimodal line, which handles text, images, audio, and video in a unified model architecture, in a faster and cheaper form factor. This is part of a broader trend across all the major labs: every top-tier model now has a corresponding 'lite' or 'flash' variant for production use cases where throughput matters more than ceiling performance. For developers building on Google's API, having a faster multimodal option is genuinely useful — many real-world applications don't need the full power of Gemini 3.5 Pro for every query. The Nano Banana naming is a bit whimsical but follows DeepMind's established internal model naming conventions. Worth noting that these releases came just days before Google DeepMind also announced Gemini 3.5 Flash's computer use capability, suggesting a rapid cadence of incremental capability rollouts rather than big-bang releases.

stories.title: Google DeepMind Brings Computer Use to Gemini 3.5 Flash
stories.summary: Google DeepMind announced that computer use — the ability for an AI model to directly interact with a computer interface, clicking, typing, and navigating like a human user — is now available in Gemini 3.5 Flash. This puts Google in direct competition with Anthropic's Claude, which has had computer use capabilities for a while, and signals that agentic AI features are becoming table stakes rather than differentiators. Computer use is one of the more consequential capability unlocks in AI right now: it's the thing that lets you actually delegate multi-step, software-mediated tasks to an AI without having to build a custom API integration for every app. The Flash variant being the delivery vehicle is interesting — it suggests Google thinks computer use needs to be fast and cheap to be practical, which is probably correct given how many low-level UI interactions a typical task might require. Security implications are real and worth thinking about: a model that can use your computer can also be manipulated through prompt injection attacks embedded in the UI it's navigating. DeepMind's blog post is light on safety specifics, which is a mild yellow flag. For developers building automation tools, this is a significant API capability to start experimenting with. The broader race to make AI agents that can actually get things done in existing software environments — without requiring every app to have an AI-native API — is heating up fast.

stories.title: LLMs Are Stuck in a Groupthink Groove — A Startup Is Trying to Fix It
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review has a sharp piece on a fundamental and underappreciated problem with current LLMs: they're not actually random when they think they are. Ask any major chatbot for a random number between 1 and 10, and you'll almost always get 7. This isn't a quirk — it's a symptom of training on human-generated data that itself reflects human cognitive biases and cultural priors. For tasks where diversity of thought matters — brainstorming, risk analysis, scientific hypothesis generation — this convergence is a real limitation. A startup is building tooling specifically designed to break LLMs out of this groove, though MIT Tech Review is cagey on implementation details. The problem is deeper than just random number generation: LLMs trained on similar data will naturally converge on similar framings, similar solutions, and similar blind spots. This is a particularly acute problem if you're using AI for anything where the goal is to find the non-obvious answer. The groupthink dynamic also compounds when multiple AI systems interact — if all agents in a multi-agent system share the same biases, you get confident convergence on wrong answers rather than useful disagreement. Yan LeCun has been making related arguments about the architectural limitations of current LLMs for a while, and this piece provides some concrete empirical texture to that critique. It's a good read for anyone who's noticed that different chatbots give suspiciously similar answers to the same question.

stories.title: OpenAI and Broadcom Unveil Jalapeño: A Custom LLM Inference Chip
stories.summary: OpenAI and Broadcom have jointly introduced a custom AI chip called Jalapeño, specifically designed for LLM inference rather than training. This is a meaningful strategic move for OpenAI: inference is where the money gets spent at scale, and depending on NVIDIA for every inference workload creates both cost and supply chain risk. Jalapeño is built to improve performance-per-watt and cost efficiency for the specific computational patterns that large language models use during inference — things like attention mechanisms and matrix multiplications at the token-generation stage. Broadcom has been quietly building a strong position in custom AI silicon alongside its networking business, and this partnership validates that strategy. For OpenAI, having its own inference silicon is part of a broader push toward vertical integration — similar to what Google did with TPUs and what Apple did with its Neural Engine. It's worth noting that training and inference have very different hardware requirements: training is about raw throughput and memory bandwidth, while inference is more about latency, batching efficiency, and cost per token. A chip optimized for inference won't help you train the next GPT model, but it could significantly reduce the cost of running the current ones. The timing — alongside the GPT-5.6 Sol preview — suggests OpenAI is thinking carefully about the economics of running increasingly capable models at consumer scale.

stories.title: AI's Volatile Power Draw Is Quietly Stressing the Electrical Grid
stories.summary: IEEE Spectrum has a sobering piece on a grid stability problem that doesn't get nearly enough attention: AI data centers don't just consume a lot of power, they consume it in highly variable, spiky patterns that are genuinely hard for grid operators to manage. Traditional industrial loads are relatively predictable — a factory runs at roughly consistent power draw during operating hours. But AI training and inference workloads can swing dramatically depending on job scheduling, model size, and batch patterns, creating sudden demand spikes that stress local grid infrastructure. This isn't purely a capacity problem (though that's real too) — it's a power quality and frequency stability problem. Grid operators are designed around the assumption that large loads change slowly and predictably. AI data centers violate that assumption. The piece notes that in some regions, local substations and distribution infrastructure are already seeing unusual stress patterns correlated with nearby data center activity. Fixes exist — better demand forecasting, on-site storage to smooth spikes, smarter job schedulers that spread load more evenly — but require coordination between AI operators and utilities that isn't happening systematically yet. As AI infrastructure continues to scale, this is going to become a serious engineering and regulatory challenge, not just an environmental one. Worth reading if you're involved in any infrastructure or energy policy work.

stories.title: Zuckerberg Admits AI Agent Development Is Going Slower Than Expected
stories.summary: Meta CEO Mark Zuckerberg has publicly acknowledged that the development of useful AI agents is proceeding more slowly than the company anticipated — a notably candid admission from someone who spent much of 2025 predicting that AI would be doing the work of mid-level engineers by now. The Reuters report is light on specifics, but the admission tracks with what a lot of practitioners are seeing: the gap between 'demo-able' agentic behavior and 'reliably deployable' agentic behavior remains frustratingly large. Agents that work great in a controlled environment tend to fail in unpredictable ways in production, especially when they encounter edge cases, ambiguous instructions, or tool failures. This matches what Simon Willison has been documenting in his sqlite-utils work — even using Fable 5 and GPT-5.5, he's finding the process requires careful prompting and human review, not set-it-and-forget-it automation. Zuckerberg's comment is also interesting context for Meta's recent AI product launches, which have been heavy on consumer-facing chat features but lighter on the autonomous agent capabilities he was hyping. The broader point is that agentic AI is genuinely hard — not because the models aren't capable of impressive things, but because real-world tasks have a long tail of exception cases that break automated pipelines. Honest calibration from a major lab CEO is actually useful signal for anyone trying to plan around AI capabilities.

stories.title: New AI Tutor Achieves Remarkable Learning Effect Sizes in Dartmouth Study
stories.summary: A newly published study from a Dartmouth course reports that a custom AI tutor achieved an effect size of 0.71 to 1.30 standard deviations on student learning outcomes — which, if it replicates, is genuinely remarkable. For context, most educational interventions that work well get effect sizes around 0.3-0.5 SD; 0.71-1.30 SD would put this in the range of one-on-one human tutoring, which is historically the gold standard and extremely expensive to scale. The study was presented at a workshop on intelligent textbooks, and the full paper is available as a PDF. The effect size range is wide, which suggests meaningful variation across students or conditions, and the study is from a single course at a single institution — replication across different subjects, student populations, and institutions is essential before drawing strong conclusions. That said, the numbers are compelling enough to take seriously. The mechanism is likely related to what educational researchers call 'mastery learning' and 'immediate feedback' — AI tutors can give students instant, personalized responses to their specific misconceptions rather than waiting for a weekly problem set grade. If this scales, it has profound implications for higher education economics and access. The question is whether the effect holds for subjects beyond whatever this Dartmouth course covered, and whether it persists in students who are less motivated or less well-prepared.

stories.title: Simon Willison's sqlite-utils 4.0 Release Candidate: A Case Study in AI-Assisted Open Source Maintenance
stories.summary: Simon Willison has been publishing a fascinating real-time case study in using frontier AI models — specifically Claude Fable 5 and GPT-5.5 — to accelerate a major open source release. The sqlite-utils 4.0 release candidate process has been unusually transparent: Willison documented that rc2 was 'mostly written by Claude Fable' at a cost of approximately $149.25 in API credits, and rc3 is now out with a growing changelog driven by working through the issue backlog with AI assistance. The project is a Python library for working with SQLite databases, widely used in the data journalism and developer tooling communities. What makes this case study valuable isn't just the 'AI wrote my code' angle — it's the granular detail about what the AI did well, where it needed guidance, and what the economics actually look like. $149 to burn through a meaningful backlog of issues and PRs is cheap by consulting standards, but it's also not free, and the human oversight required is still substantial. One of the interesting tips Willison shared from an AI Engineer World's Fair fireside chat: let Fable use its own judgment rather than over-specifying how it should work. For example, rather than telling it exactly how to write tests, let it decide when automated testing is warranted for a given feature. This kind of meta-learning about how to collaborate with AI coding assistants is increasingly valuable and hard to come by.

stories.title: Amazon Is Shutting Down Mechanical Turk to New Customers — Likely a Death Knell for the Platform
stories.summary: Amazon has announced it will stop accepting new customers for Mechanical Turk, its long-running crowdsourced human intelligence task platform — a move that TechCrunch describes as potentially signaling 'the last days' of MTurk. Mechanical Turk launched in 2005 and became a foundational part of the AI research infrastructure, used extensively for generating training data, running behavioral experiments, and doing the kind of human evaluation work that's hard to automate. The platform has been in slow decline for years, squeezed by competition from newer annotation platforms and, increasingly, by AI tools that can handle tasks that previously required human judgment. Closing to new customers while continuing to serve existing ones is a classic managed wind-down pattern. For researchers and teams that still rely on MTurk, this is a planning signal to start migrating to alternatives like Scale AI, Surge HI, or cloud-based annotation pipelines. The deeper story here is that MTurk was always a somewhat exploitative platform — workers (called 'Turkers') were typically paid well below minimum wage for piecework tasks, and there was no employment relationship or protections. The irony of an AI training data platform being displaced by the AI it helped train is not lost on anyone. This is worth noting for anyone in the ML community who builds human evaluation pipelines.

stories.title: Alibaba Bans Employees from Using Claude Code, Classifies It as High-Risk
stories.summary: Alibaba has reportedly banned its employees from using Anthropic's Claude Code, classifying it as high-risk software — a decision that has practical implications for the AI coding tool market and raises interesting questions about enterprise AI security. The specific concern is almost certainly data exfiltration: Claude Code, like most AI coding assistants, sends code context to external servers, and for a company like Alibaba that would mean sending proprietary code to an American company's infrastructure. This is a standard enterprise security concern, not paranoia — most large tech companies have policies restricting which AI tools employees can use with internal code, precisely because of IP and data privacy risks. What makes this notable is that it's Alibaba specifically, a major tech company that has its own AI models (Qwen) and would reasonably prefer employees use internal tools. The 'high-risk' classification suggests this was a formal security review decision rather than an informal ban, which means there are probably specific threat model concerns being articulated internally. For Anthropic, enterprise sales to Chinese tech companies was probably never a major revenue line, but this signals a general dynamic where non-Western enterprises will increasingly default to local AI tools for anything involving proprietary data. It also highlights a real gap in Claude Code's enterprise story: it needs a robust on-premises or private cloud deployment option to compete in security-sensitive environments.

stories.title: Does Code Cleanliness Actually Help AI Coding Agents? A Controlled Study Says Yes
stories.summary: A new paper on arXiv (score 108 on HN, which indicates genuine community interest) runs a controlled minimal-pair study asking whether code cleanliness — things like variable naming, function decomposition, and comment quality — affects how well AI coding agents perform on that codebase. This is a practically important question that most teams haven't thought to ask: if you're using Claude Code or Copilot on a messy legacy codebase, does the mess hurt the AI's performance beyond just being annoying for humans? The answer, not surprisingly, appears to be yes — AI agents perform measurably better on cleaner code, which has implications for teams thinking about how to prepare their codebases for AI-assisted development. The methodology uses 'minimal pairs' — functionally equivalent code samples that differ only in cleanliness characteristics — to isolate the effect. This is the right experimental design and gives the results more credibility than anecdotal comparisons. The practical implication for teams: investing in code quality pays dividends not just for human developers but also for the AI tools operating on that code. This creates a new and somewhat compelling argument for technical debt reduction that goes beyond the usual 'it'll make your engineers happier' framing. Worth reading if you're making decisions about where to invest engineering effort in an AI-augmented development environment.

stories.title: Agility Robotics Goes Public via SPAC — With Unusually Honest Expectations
stories.summary: Agility Robotics, the humanoid robot company backed by Amazon, is going public via a SPAC — and its CEO is being notably candid about the timeline for consumer-facing robots, saying don't hold your breath. This is a refreshing contrast to the hype-heavy valuation narratives coming from competitors like Figure and Physical Intelligence, who have been raising at eye-watering valuations while promising near-term transformations of the physical labor market. Agility's realistic positioning is that humanoid robots will first be useful in structured industrial environments — warehouses, factories — where the tasks are repetitive and the environment can be engineered around the robot's capabilities. The SPAC route is interesting: SPACs have largely fallen out of fashion after the 2021 boom-and-bust cycle, and using one in 2026 suggests either that traditional IPO markets weren't available at an acceptable valuation or that speed-to-public was a priority. Amazon's involvement as a backer and early customer (Agility's Digit robot has been tested in Amazon fulfillment centers) gives the company a degree of commercial credibility that pure-play humanoid startups lack. The honest messaging about consumer timelines is strategically smart: it sets realistic expectations with institutional investors who got burned on SPAC hype before. Whether the underlying technology is ready for the industrial deployment they're betting on is the real question.

stories.title: Trump Memecoin Investors Lost $3.8 Billion While Trump Made $636 Million
stories.summary: An analysis has found that roughly 1 million people who bought the $TRUMP memecoin have collectively lost $3.8 billion, while President Trump himself netted approximately $636 million from the venture. The math here is not a coincidence — it's the basic structure of how memecoins work, with early holders (and particularly the issuer) benefiting while later retail buyers absorb losses as the price declines from its peak. The $TRUMP memecoin launched around Trump's inauguration and saw frenzied buying, particularly from overseas crypto investors who may have believed buying the coin would generate political goodwill or regulatory favor. That specific dynamic — foreign nationals buying an asset tied to a sitting US president — has attracted congressional scrutiny and ethics questions that remain unresolved. The $636 million figure is the kind of number that tends to land in congressional testimony and regulatory filings, so this analysis will likely have a longer life than a typical crypto postmortem. For the crypto industry, this is an ongoing PR problem: it's hard to argue for regulatory legitimacy when the most visible recent coin associated with the US government is a documented wealth transfer from retail investors to a political figure. The analysis was covered by TechCrunch but is worth noting for anyone tracking the intersection of crypto regulation, political ethics, and retail investor protection.

stories.title: When AI Costs More Than the Engineer: The Breakeven Problem
stories.summary: A post from Tom Tunguz making rounds on Hacker News examines a question that's becoming increasingly relevant for engineering organizations: at what point does AI API spend exceed the cost of just hiring the engineer you're trying to replace or augment? Tunguz puts the crossover point around 2029 based on current AI cost trajectories and engineer salary benchmarks, though that projection obviously depends heavily on assumptions about model pricing trends and engineer compensation. The practical implication for today is that AI coding tools are still well within 'augmentation economics' — they cost far less than the engineer hours they save. But the math shifts when you're talking about fully autonomous AI agents doing production engineering work at scale, where the compute costs compound rapidly with task complexity. This is the kind of analysis that CFOs and engineering VPs should be running as they plan AI budgets: there's a real risk that organizations spin up AI-augmented workflows without doing the unit economics carefully, then get surprised when the API bill arrives. The analysis is particularly relevant for startups that have been treating AI API costs as a negligible line item — at scale, they very much aren't. As always with Tom Tunguz, the post is data-informed and worth reading even if you disagree with the specific projection.

stories.title: Google Ordered to Pay €4.1 Billion Fine for Using Android to Block Rivals
stories.summary: The EU's antitrust case against Google's Android practices has concluded with a final order to pay a €4.1 billion fine, one of the largest antitrust penalties in tech history. The case centered on Google's practice of requiring device manufacturers to pre-install Google Search and the Chrome browser as a condition of licensing Android's app store, effectively locking out rival search engines and browsers on the dominant mobile platform. Google's spokesperson responded by saying the judgment 'fails to recognize' the company's 'significant investment to ensure Android remains open' — a defense that has not been particularly persuasive to European regulators, who view the mandatory bundling as structurally anti-competitive regardless of Android's open-source status. This case has been grinding through the European courts for years, and the final payment order marks a significant milestone. The fine is real money even for Google, but the more consequential outcome may be behavioral: the remedies imposed alongside the fine have changed how Android licensing works in Europe, creating more space for alternative browsers and search engines to reach users. For the broader tech industry, this case is a reference point in ongoing EU Digital Markets Act enforcement, which applies similar logic to a wider range of 'gatekeeper' behaviors. US regulators are watching European antitrust enforcement closely as they develop their own approaches to platform competition.

stories.title: Parents Warned Not to Publicly Share Children's Images Amid AI Abuse Risks
stories.summary: The UK's National Crime Agency has issued a warning to parents about the growing risk of children's publicly shared images being used to generate child sexual abuse material (CSAM) using AI image generation tools. This is a deeply serious and underreported problem: as AI image generation has become more accessible and capable, the barrier to creating synthetic CSAM from real photos of real children has dropped dramatically. The NCA warning is a public awareness measure, but it also signals that law enforcement is seeing actual cases where publicly available family photos have been used this way. The technical challenge for platforms is significant: detecting AI-generated synthetic CSAM is harder than detecting previously known material, because hash-matching systems (the primary tool currently used) don't work for novel generated content. The policy challenge is equally difficult: the images used as source material are legal (normal family photos), and the harm is in the generation step rather than the collection step. For parents, the practical advice is to reconsider the default assumption that sharing children's photos publicly on social media is harmless. For platform companies, this is going to become a major content moderation and liability issue as regulators in multiple jurisdictions look at AI-facilitated CSAM as a legislative priority.

stories.title: Midjourney Seeks to Compel Hollywood Studios to Disclose Their Own AI Usage
stories.summary: In an ongoing legal dispute with three major Hollywood studios over alleged copyright infringement, Midjourney has flipped the script by seeking to compel the studios to disclose how they themselves use AI internally. This is a classic litigation countermove: if the studios are suing Midjourney for training on copyrighted content, Midjourney's legal team wants to establish whether those same studios are using AI tools that do similar things. The discovery request is legally interesting because it puts the studios in an uncomfortable position — acknowledging significant internal AI use might undercut the 'AI is harmful to creators' narrative that underpins their suit, while denying it risks being contradicted by internal communications. Hollywood has been deeply ambivalent about AI: publicly, the major studios have supported writers' and actors' demands for AI protections; privately, they've been exploring (and in some cases deploying) AI for visual effects, background generation, script coverage, and other production tasks. The Midjourney move is smart strategy regardless of how the underlying copyright questions get resolved. It also highlights the broader hypocrisy tension in the AI copyright debate, where many of the most vocal critics of AI training data practices are simultaneously users of AI tools. This case is one to watch closely for anyone in the creative industry or working on AI IP policy.

stories.title: A Martian Rock Has Unexplained Carbon — Biology Is One Possible Explanation
stories.summary: Ars Technica covers a genuinely intriguing finding: a Martian rock analyzed by the Perseverance rover shows an unusual abundance of carbon in a form and location that scientists can't yet fully explain. Carbon is not inherently exotic on Mars — it shows up in various geological and atmospheric processes — but the specific signature here has researchers considering biological processes among the potential explanations, alongside non-biological alternatives like volcanic outgassing, UV photolysis of CO2, and meteoritic delivery. The scientific community is being appropriately cautious: 'biology could explain this' is not the same as 'we found evidence of life,' and there are documented non-biological pathways that could produce similar signatures. What makes this worth paying attention to is that it's not a one-off anomaly — there have been several Perseverance findings over the past year where the carbon chemistry is puzzling enough to warrant serious alternative hypothesis testing. The Mars Sample Return mission, despite its budget and timeline troubles, would be the right tool for resolving this kind of ambiguity — definitive isotopic analysis requires lab equipment that can't be miniaturized enough to fly to Mars. For now, this is a 'watch this space' story, but it's the kind of ambiguous signal that keeps astrobiologists up at night in the best possible way.

stories.title: A Device That Revives Donor Eyeballs Could Finally Make Eye Transplants Possible
stories.summary: MIT Technology Review covers a significant medical device development: researchers have built a system that can maintain and partially revive eyeballs from deceased donors, potentially enabling whole-eye transplants that have previously been impossible. The core problem with eye transplantation has been twofold: the surgery is technically difficult, and donor eyes begin degenerating immediately after death, giving surgeons an extremely narrow window to work with. The new device appears to extend that window and improve the condition of the eye going into surgery. The historical precedent here is sobering — when whole-eye transplants were attempted previously, the transplanted eye could not restore vision, likely because the optic nerve connections required for sight are even harder to re-establish than the vascular connections. The researchers believe that better eye preservation is a necessary precondition for solving the vision-restoration problem, not a sufficient one on its own. This is the kind of incremental enabling technology that doesn't make headlines on its own but is required for a downstream breakthrough to become possible. For the millions of people with vision loss from conditions like corneal disease, glaucoma damage, or retinal disorders, the possibility of whole-eye transplantation is meaningful — though clinical translation from 'we can keep an eye viable longer' to 'we can transplant an eye that lets someone see' is still years away.
